narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your full profile, including your early academic rankings, LSAT score, family income bracket, and planned full-time enrollment, and it calculated an 80% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this tool to prioritize admits likely to pass and succeed in practice. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking—all of which worked against you by signaling lower bar passage odds compared to stronger performers. Specifically, your Year 3 decile of 1 (well below the average of 5.53) had the strongest negative pull, as students in higher deciles typically show the consistent performance needed for bar success. Your LSAT score of 19.30, far under the dataset average of 36.70, was the next major drag, since higher scores strongly correlate with passing the bar in historical data. Even your Year 1 decile of 1 (versus an average of 5.71) added to the concern, suggesting early academic struggles that carried forward. While your lower-middle family income and full-time status (common among 92.7% of students) didn't sway things much, they couldn't offset these core academic red flags. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often have mid-to-high deciles and LSATs above 35, your profile aligned more with those who historically struggled on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from your background—many overcome similar starts—but the model flags patterns from real outcomes. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35 or higher, strengthening your recent grades to climb those deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources early. Reapplying with those improvements could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools using this system. You're close in areas like GPA; building on that resilience will pay off.
